communications.communication:
Dear Requester,

Thank you for your message. I’d like to assist you, but your email did not include complete sentences, specific questions, or what public records request you are referring to. I am unable to ascertain what clarification you’re seeking.

Please note that the invoice you received is already itemized. It includes a breakdown of applicable fees in accordance with RCW 42.56.120, such as scanning, electronic delivery, and media costs.

You have indicated a preference not to utilize the JustFOIA portal. As an alternative, we can provide the responsive records to you on a USB drive, which may be sent to you via certified Certified Mail with Return Receipt (CMRR) or picked up in-person at our office. If you would like to receive the USB drive via CMRR, please provide your full name and mailing address. If you would like to pick up the USB drive in our office, please arrange a date and time.

If you elect to receive a USB drive, you are responsible for the actual hardware cost. If you elect to receive a USB drive via CMRR, you are responsible for the actual hardware and mailing cost.  If you choose either of these options, we will send you an updated invoice.

 

Sincerely,

Public Records Office

Lake Washington School District

publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org<mailto:publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org>

communications.subject: RE: Washington Public Records Act Request #PRR-2024-109
communications.communication:
October 01, 2025

Public Records Officer
Lake Washington School District No. 414
16250 NE 74th Street
Redmond, WA 98052

via email to PublicRecordsRequest@lwsd.org

Re: Request for Clarification - Itemization of Fee Calculation – Missing Links to productions installments (if any) - Public Records Act (RCW 42.56)

Dear Public Records Officer,

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.120(2)(f), agencies must provide “an itemized statement of the costs” before imposing fees, so that the requestor may meaningfully evaluate, clarify, or narrow the request. Your dated agency invoice must satisfy this requirement and disclose the basis for per-file or per-gigabyte charges.

Additionally, pursuant to RCW 42.56 and applicable case law requiring agencies to conduct an adequate and reasonably calculated search (Neighborhood Alliance v. Spokane County, 172 Wn.2d 702 (2011)), the agency must provide a detailed description of the digital location and digital container of all responsive electronic records.

This includes, but is not limited to:

The system or platform in which the records are stored (e.g., Microsoft Exchange, Office 365, SharePoint, GovQA, JustFOIA, local file servers, network drives, or cloud storage).

The specific digital container where the records reside (e.g., individual Outlook .pst archive, Exchange mailbox, folder structure, database repository, or compressed .zip archive).

The path or directory structure used by the agency to locate the records (e.g., \\Server\PublicRecords\EmailArchives\CustodianName.pst).

Identification of the custodian(s) or account(s) associated with each container.

Whether responsive records were located in native format (e.g., .pst, .msg, .eml, .docx, .xlsx, .pdf) or were exported/transformed into another format for production.

Accordingly, I demand that your agency provide a detailed invoice itemization for public records fees  including:

1. A complete electronic copy of your dated agency fee invoice for delivery of electronic copies of public records responsive to this request.
2. A complete list of responsive record file names, organized in the manner maintained by the agency;
3. A detailed description of the location and digital container where the records reside, and the path or directory structure used by the agency to locate the records, as well as the name and description of the system or platform in which the records are stored and whether responsive records were located in native format.
4. The electronic file size of each responsive record;
5. The calculation method used to determine the charges assessed (per-file, per-gigabyte, or otherwise); and
6. Identification of any additional factors or “actual costs” the agency contends justify the fees.

This disclosure is legally necessary to comply with RCW 42.56.120 and RCW 42.56.100 (requiring agencies to provide the “fullest assistance” to requestors). Without the file names and sizes, I cannot verify the accuracy of the agency’s invoice nor determine whether narrowing the request would reduce costs.

Alternative Calculation – Bundled / Compressed Production

Further, I specifically request that your agency provide a separate fee calculation and invoice detailing the total costs if the responsive files were produced in bundled or compressed format (e.g., .zip archives or native .pst file) rather than as unbundled individual files.

This request is grounded in:

•	RCW 42.56.080(2): Records must be provided in the format requested if “reasonably translatable.”

•	WAC 44-14-050(2): Agencies must produce electronic records in the requested format if it is reasonable and feasible.

•	RCW 42.56.100: Agencies must provide the “fullest assistance” to requestors and may not impose artificial barriers or inflated costs.

Bundling into .zip files (or producing in native format) is technologically trivial, reduces storage needs, and directly reduces per-file charges under RCW 42.56.120. Refusing to provide this option while inflating fees by multiplying file counts is inconsistent with the PRA’s requirements and case law (see Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706 (1989)).

If the agency continues to withhold this information, the result is a denial of access under RCW 42.56.550, as the PRA does not authorize the assessment of vague or unsupported fees. Inflated or unsubstantiated charges are unlawful, and penalties may accrue for each day of violation in addition to attorney fees and costs. See Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706 (1989).

Unlawful Charges for Staff Time

If the agency’s invoice includes costs for search, review, or redaction time, those are expressly prohibited.

•	RCW 42.56.120(2)(a): Agencies may not charge for staff time spent on redacting, reviewing, or locating records.

•	WAC 44-14-07003(2): Staff time is nonrecoverable.

Demand: Identify any portion of the invoice reflecting staff time and immediately remove such charges.

Adequacy of Search

Agencies must conduct a search “reasonably calculated to uncover all responsive documents.”

•	Neighborhood Alliance v. Spokane County, 172 Wn.2d 702 (2011).

Demand: Identify custodians searched, search terms used, systems queried (including backups, archives, and third-party portals), and confirm that no records were excluded based on convenience or cost.

Tracking Installments & Production Integrity

Your agency must produce records in proper installments that preserve identity and accessibility. The request explicitly forbade requiring login, passwords, registration, or portal access. It demanded records be delivered by direct email attachments or open cloud links in native format. I have not received any such delivery. Any “security key” or link requiring a portal violates the request’s terms and undermines meaningful access. If the security key or portal is used to limit or condition access (e.g., enforcing a time window to “claim” records), or to otherwise forfeit access, that imposes an impermissible forfeiture condition not authorized by the PRA.

•	RCW 42.56.080(3): Agencies must provide installment responses.

•	Broken links, overwritten installments, or failure to identify installment contents are violations.

Demand: Provide a production log of all installments, with dates, delivery methods, and content description. Provide direct, no-login/no-registration links or email attachments for all records and records productions produced to date, in their native electronic format, consistent with the original request. Provide a detailed installment schedule for remaining records (with dates and record groupings). Finally, provide a complete, PRA-compliant exemption log for all withheld or redacted records, in compliance with RCW 42.56.27(3).

Preservation Notice

Because litigation is anticipated, the agency is on notice of its duty to preserve all responsive records.

Demand: Implement an immediate litigation hold on all responsive data, including metadata, emails, attachments, backups, and internal communications related to this request. Failure to preserve will be treated as spoliation of evidence. Confirm that preservation holds are in place to prevent alteration or destruction of any responsive records pending final resolution.

Reservation of Rights and Judicial Review

Failure to cure the above deficiencies will be deemed a denial of access under RCW 42.56.550. Please be advised:

•	Statutory penalties accrue per day per record unlawfully withheld.

•	Courts have broad discretion to impose penalties where agencies act in bad faith (Yousoufian v. Office of Ron Sims, 168 Wn.2d 444 (2010)).

•	Attorney fees and costs are mandatory upon judicial review under RCW 42.56.550(4).

Abandonment, expiration, or closure  of this public records request prior to full compliance with RCW 42.56.100 and RCW 42.56.520 is unlawful. The Public Records Act expressly requires agencies to assist requestors in clarification, prohibits premature closure, and mandates retention until resolution. Failure to comply will subject the District to statutory penalties and judicial enforcement under RCW 42.56.550.

I request written compliance within seven business days. If the agency fails to cure, I will seek judicial review and pursue all remedies, including statutory penalties, attorney fees, and sanctions for spoliation.

Thank you.

 Lake Washington School District is still in the process of searching for additional records. The district is currently experiencing an unusually high volume of extensive and complex public records requests. We anticipate being able to send you the next installment by 11/12/2025. 
  
 Please be sure to download and save a copy of the records for your use. communications.subject: RE: Washington Public Records Act Request #PRR-2024-109
communications.communication:
Request for Clarification and Formal Letter of Objection

Subject: Request for Clarification and Objection to Production Format – Request PRR-2024-119 – Cure Required
To: Public Records Officer Lake Washington School District 
Date: December 18, 2025
Re: Improper "52 Card Pickup" Production of Records for PRR-2024-119

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing to formally object to the format of the installments your agency has provided regarding my request for "Public Records Requests received by your agency in the year 2023 and... your agency's Responses to each."

The Defects in Production The District has provided a "response letter" listing various request descriptions (e.g., Request Date 2/28/2023 regarding janitorial supplies, Request Date 8/12/2023 regarding safety net staffing) and a separate, unorganized "dump" of attachment files (e.g., PO 1000652825 Waxie.pdf, RE_ LWSD_LWEA...pdf).

This production method—often referred to in litigation as "52 Card Pickup"—severs the parent-child relationship between the Request/Response and the Responsive Documents.

By separating the documents from the specific request they belong to, the District has failed to produce the "Response" as a complete record. A "Response" consists of the transmittal message linked to the documents provided. The current production forces the requestor to guess which loose attachment corresponds to which request description.

Violations of the Public Records Act

1.	Failure to Provide Fullest Assistance (RCW 42.56.100): Providing unassociated, disorganized files that require the requestor to reconstruct the agency's data structure violates the statutory duty to provide fullest assistance.
2.	Alteration of Records: The original "Responses" sent in 2023 were likely emails or portal clusters containing specific attachments. By stripping these attachments out and presenting them as loose files, the District has altered the native format and integrity of the records requested.

Demand for Cure and Order of Production

I demand that you cure the defects in these installments immediately. My original request explicitly stated: "I request a complete electronic copy of all Public Records Requests received by your agency... and a complete electronic copy of your agency's Responses to each".

The current production—a loose "dump" of unassociated files—fails to provide a "complete electronic copy" of a Response. A "Response" is a transaction consisting of a specific inquiry linked to specific provided documents. By stripping the documents (such as the Waxie Purchase Order or the Safety Net emails ) away from the context of the request they satisfy, you have altered the record and failed to produce it in accordance with the clear terms of my request.

To cure this violation, please re-produce this installment (and organize all future installments) in a format that maintains the strict association between the Request/Response and the specific documents provided for that transaction.

Required Format: Organized Folder Structure To comply with the request for "complete electronic copies" of these transactions, you must utilize a folder structure that preserves the integrity of the records:

Folder Naming: Create a unique digital folder for each distinct Public Records Request received (e.g., a folder named "PRR-2023-02-28-Janitorial" or "PRR-2023-08-12-SafetyNet").

Folder Contents: Within each folder, you must include:

The Original Request: A digital copy of the actual request received by the agency (e.g., the email or portal entry from the citizen), as explicitly demanded in Item 1 of my request.

The Responsive Records: The specific files provided to that citizen (e.g., the Waxie PO must be located inside the "Janitorial" folder; the Kelly Pease emails  must be located inside the "Safety Net" folder).

The Agency Response: The cover letter or email sent by the agency to the citizen.

This structure is the only way to fulfill the request for "Responses"  rather than a disorganized collection of raw data.

communications.subject: RE: Washington Public Records Act Request #PRR-2024-109
communications.communication:
Dear Requester,

Thank you for your email regarding request PRR-2024-109. Your original request stated:

1. I request a complete electronic copy of all Public Records Requests received by your agency in the year 2023 and a complete electronic copy of your agency's Responses to each of these Public Records Requests for the year 2023.

Note: Parents and students seeking GRADES and/or TRANSCRIPTS in the year 2023 can be excluded from this request.

To fulfill your request, we interpreted “all Public Records Requests received by your agency in the year 2023” to mean the District’s official public records log, which contains the full content of each request and response as maintained by the agency. This log is the authoritative record of all 2023 requests and responses. If you intended to receive the original submissions (such as individual emails or portal entries) instead of the log, please let us know, and we will amend the scope immediately.

We understand your concern about identifying files in the latest installment. Going forward, we will make sure to provide an email with a list of the file names for the installment. Please see the list below for the most recent installment provided 12/15/2025:

Omnia Contract 202239-01.pdf
PO 1000652825 Waxie.pdf
RE_ Special Education Letter to Families of Stu....pdf
RE_ Special Education Letter to Families of Stu...(1).pdf
FW_ Letters for Translating.pdf
Safety Net Programming at FHMS.pdf
RE_ LWSD_LWEA Bargain Team Request....pdf
Letters for Translating.pdf
Mail.pdf
Hoard - Log of Documents.pdf
Accepted_ Safety Net Programming at FHMS(2).pdf
Accepted_ Safety Net Programming at FHMS(1).pdf

No records have been altered. They are provided as maintained in the District’s system and attachments are preserved in their original format.

If you prefer the records in a bundled zip file or in native email format please let us know. Currently, JustFOIA does not support folder organization but we can offer alternatives such as bundled installments. Please note, if any exemptions or redactions are applied, they will be detailed in an exemption log within the installment.

To ensure we meet your expectations please advise if you would prefer all records delivered at once in a single zip file after the district completes its searches, reviews, and redactions of if the District should continue with installments as records are located.

Please let us know your preferred format and delivery method so we can proceed accordingly. Thank you for your email; we look forward to hearing from you. Please reach out with any additional questions.

Thank you,

Public Records Office

Lake Washington School District

publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org<mailto:publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org<mailto:publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org%3cmailto:publicrecordsrequest@lwsd.org>>

title: Public surveillance records request (Texas Department of Public Safety)

communications.subject: Texas Public Information Act Request: Public surveillance records request (Texas Department of Public Safety)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Texas Public Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

1.	⁠All policies and procedures governing how your agency handles public records requests related to:
	⁠•	⁠Flock Safety systems
	⁠•	⁠License Plate Reader (LPR) technology
	⁠•	⁠Automatic License Plate Reader (ALPR) systems
	⁠•	⁠Any other license plate recognition or surveillance technology

2.	⁠Any guidelines, instructions, or training materials provided to staff regarding the processing of public records requests for the above-mentioned technologies.

3.	⁠Any written procedures for redacting, exempting, or withholding records related to license plate reader systems.

If any portion of this request is denied, please provide a written explanation citing the specific statutory exemption for each denial.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Daniel Derfus

title: Sheriff use of force data (2017-2025) (St. Johns County Sheriff's Office)

communications.subject: Florida Sunshine Law Request: Sheriff use of force data (2017-2025) (St. Johns County Sheriff's Office)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Florida Sunshine Law, I hereby request the following records:

Use of force (UOF) data dating from January 1, 2017 to December 31, 2025. If possible, please provide this data in database or spreadsheet format and include the case number, incident date, incident time, subject injured indicator, subject injury force related info (e.g. Yes or No), subject age, subject gender, subject ethnicity, subject weapon type, incident area occurred, force type, officer division (e.g. law enforcement operations, detention/correctional operations, etc.), badge number and names associated with the officers involved. If one or more of these fields or dates is unavailable, provide all fields and/or information that is available responsive to my request. If no database or spreadsheet format is available, please provide copies of each individual use of force report or other report where use of force information is documented/reported by officers. Note, this is for all use of force incidents reported by your agency, not limited to only those incidents required to be reported under state or federal programs.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Samuel Sinyangwe

Samuel Sinyangwe 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2201921/ Thank you for contacting the Records Unit of the St. Johns County Sheriff’s Office. 
 
We are in receipt of your records request. 
 
You can expect an email response from our unit representative, as email is our primary means of contact. 
Response times may vary for requests submitted outside of regular business hours, on weekends, or holidays. 
 
We want to inform you in advance that our public records request fees will change effective Thursday , January 1st. 
Please review the attached fee schedule: Public Records fees 
 
St. Johns County Background Check: $10 
 
Crash Reports $10 (Free for involved parties only) 
 
A service charge will apply for public records request assistance that exceeds 15 minutes. 
 
This “shall be based on the cost incurred for such extensive use of information technology resources or the labor cost of the personnel providing the service that is actually incurred by the agency or attributable to the agency for the clerical and supervisory assistance required, or both.”  Florida Statute 119.07(4)(d) 
 
For requests requiring more than 15 minutes of personnel time (for locating, redacting, reviewing, and preparing), a special service charge will be applied of $50 for the first hour and $75 each additional hour. 
  
If any of the above factors change your request, you can reply to this email with the modifications you would like to make.

title: DOJ Voter Data Agreements (Secretary of State of Kentucky)

communications.subject: Kentucky Open Records Act Request: DOJ Voter Data Agreements (Secretary of State of Kentucky)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Kentucky Open Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

1. Any and all memoranda of understanding (MOUs), agreements, contracts, or similar instruments between the state and the U.S. Department of Justice relating to the provision of voter registration data, voter files, or voter roll information, from January 20, 2025 to the present.

2. Any draft, unsigned, proposed, or rejected versions of such agreements.

3. Any amendments, addenda, or modifications to such agreements.

This request includes but is not limited to any document described or labeled as a "memorandum of understanding" or contract relating to voter data, voter list maintenance, or voter roll verification.

If any records are withheld in whole or in part, please identify the specific statutory exemption relied upon for each withholding.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Audrey Nielsen

title: Braxton County, WV Opioid Settlement Funds Spring 2026

communications.subject: West Virginia Freedom of Information Act Request: Braxton County, WV Opioid Settlement Funds Spring 2026
communications.communication:
Ariana Davidson
1511 University Avenue, P.O. Box 6010
Morgantown, WV 26506-6010
March 12th 2026

Sue Ann Rutherford, County Clerk
300 Main St #109, Sutton, WV 26601

Dear Sue Ann Rutherford,
Under the West Virginia Freedom of Information Act, §29-B-1-1 et seq., I am requesting an opportunity to inspect or obtain copies of public records that pertain to Braxton County opioid settlement funds. Specifically, I am requesting: 
- Documentation of the amount of funds the county has received from the federal opioid lawsuit settlements from March 1, 2025 to March 12th, 2026. 
- The application forms that any organization would submit to receive settlement funds
- Documentation of how much county-level funding has been awarded from March 1, 2025 to March 12th, 2026. 
- Any completed application of an organization that has received settlement funds from the county from from March 1, 2025 to March 12th, 2026. 
- Documentation of the process by which funds are awarded
- Any documents that contain the names of the individuals who review the funding applications and determine the awards
- Any documents that account for future plans from the county for the use of opioid settlement funds
If there are any fees for searching or copying these records, please inform me if the cost will exceed $25. However, I would also like to request a waiver of all fees in that the disclosure of the requested information is in the public interest and will contribute significantly to the public’s understanding of how the opioid settlement funds are being spent. I am a student journalist whose request is strictly for news-gathering purposes. This information is not being sought for commercial purposes. 
The West Virginia Freedom of Information Act requires a response to this request be made within five business days. If access to the records I am requesting will take longer than this amount of time, please contact me with information about when I might expect copies or the ability to inspect the requested records. 

If you deny any or all of this request, please cite each specific exemption you feel justifies the refusal to release the information and notify me of the appeal procedures available to me under the law. 
Thank you for considering my request. 
Sincerely, Ariana Davidson
arianajdavidson@icloud.com

communications.subject: Re: West Virginia Freedom of Information Act Request: Braxton County, WV Opioid Settlement Funds Spring 2026
communications.communication: I have attached copies of the three checks we received for the opioid fund.  We have not spent any of the funds.  Any forms or other info you were requesting will  need to be directed to the County Commission.

title: Request on Political Appointees (Consumer Financial Protection Bureau)

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Request on Political Appointees (Consumer Financial Protection Bureau)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

This is a request under the Freedom of Information Act. I hereby request the following records:

1. A list of any and all political appointments at the agency appointed from January 20, 2025 until the date of your final response to this request.

2. Copies of resumes for any and all political appointees hired since January 20, 2025 until the date of your final response to this request. These records are likely in the possession of the Designated Agency Ethics Official (DAEO).

Please provide these records as available on a rolling basis.

Documented is an investigative watchdog and reporting group that explores the relationship between special interests and government. This request is likely to contribute significantly to the public’s understanding of the operations of the government. Disclosure of the requested records serves the public interest, and this request is not being made for any commercial purposes. Therefore, I am requesting a waiver of any fees. If you do not grant my fee waiver request, please notify me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request be filled electronically by email attachment or other electronic file transfer if available, or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter.  I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,
Nina

title: Face recognition agreements, training, and use materials (Tulsa Police Department)

communications.subject: Oklahoma Open Records Act Request: Face recognition agreements, training, and use materials (Tulsa Police Department)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

This is a public records request being submitted under Zackary DeCicco.

I hereby request the following records related to this agency’s use of any face recognition platform, including but not limited to any system over which this agency does not maintain primary ownership but to which members of the agency have access or any platform that enables face recognition:

1. AGREEMENTS and CONTRACTS
Any and all agreements (including related amendments, attachments, and exhibits) related to the acquisition and use of this "software," algorithm, or program.

2. INSTRUCTIONAL MATERIALS and TRAINING
All instructional materials, presentations and presentation materials and other guidance on the use of "the software." Responsive materials include recorded video and audio, PowerPoint files, prepared remarks, and slides. This also includes training material governing the use, sharing, or access to the "software" or any data related to or collected by the face recognition software/technology, including the legal standard that is required before using the technology.

3. PRIVACY ASSESSMENTS
A copy of any data retention guidelines or policies or other materials evaluating or guiding the security of the "software" or the privacy of the data involved.

4. USE POLICIES 
Please provide a copy of any policy directives, guidance documents, memoranda, training materials, or similar records governing the use and function of this technology for any law enforcement purpose. Responsive materials include all those related to data retention, permissible and impermissible use, and security standards.

5. USE LOGS
All logs documenting the use of and searched conducted using the face recognition system for the period from August 1, 2025 through February 23, 2026. 

This is a request being made in the public interest and not for commercial motives.

If your office anticipates that it will need to charge fees for the processing of this request, please inform me of the estimated costs to fulfill this request in advance of fully processing this request.

Please provide these materials to me electronically, if possible.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response in accordance with the dictates of the public records law.

Sincerely,

Zackary DeCicco

Dear Zackary DeCicco: 
Thank you for your interest in public records of the Tulsa Police Department. Your request has been received and is being processed in accordance with Title 51 of the Oklahoma State Statutes, Subsection 24, also known as the Oklahoma Open Records Act. Your request was received in this office on 2/25/2026 and given the reference number P002882-022526 for tracking purposes. Records Requested: To Whom It May Concern: 

This is a public records request being submitted under Zackary DeCicco. 

I hereby request the following records related to this agency’s use of any face recognition platform, including but not limited to any system over which this agency does not maintain primary ownership but to which members of the agency have access or any platform that enables face recognition: 

1. AGREEMENTS and CONTRACTS 
Any and all agreements (including related amendments, attachments, and exhibits) related to the acquisition and use of this "software," algorithm, or program. 

2. INSTRUCTIONAL MATERIALS and TRAINING 
All instructional materials, presentations and presentation materials and other guidance on the use of "the software." Responsive materials include recorded video and audio, PowerPoint files, prepared remarks, and slides. This also includes training material governing the use, sharing, or access to the "software" or any data related to or collected by the face recognition software/technology, including the legal standard that is required before using the technology. 

3. PRIVACY ASSESSMENTS 
A copy of any data retention guidelines or policies or other materials evaluating or guiding the security of the "software" or the privacy of the data involved. 

4. USE POLICIES 
Please provide a copy of any policy directives, guidance documents, memoranda, training materials, or similar records governing the use and function of this technology for any law enforcement purpose. Responsive materials include all those related to data retention, permissible and impermissible use, and security standards. 

5. USE LOGS 
All logs documenting the use of and searched conducted using the face recognition system for the period from August 1, 2025 through February 23, 2026. 

This is a request being made in the public interest and not for commercial motives. 

If your office anticipates that it will need to charge fees for the processing of this request, please inform me of the estimated costs to fulfill this request in advance of fully processing this request. 

Please provide these materials to me electronically, if possible. 

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response in accordance with the dictates of the public records law. 

Sincerely, 

Zackary DeCicco 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2225453/ 
Your request will be forwarded to the relevant department(s) to locate the information you seek and to determine the volume and any costs associated with satisfying your request. You will be contacted about the availability and/or provided with copies of the records in question. PLEASE NOTE: Title 51 of the Oklahoma State Statutes, Subsection 24, also known as the Oklahoma Open Records Act does not require a governmental body to create new information, to do legal research, or to answer questions. 
You can monitor the progress of your request at the link below and you'll receive an email when your request has been completed. Again, thank you for using the Open Records Center. 
Tulsa Police Department

title: Joseph Alfred McNeil (March 25, 1942 – September 4, 2025)

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Joseph Alfred McNeil (March 25, 1942 – September 4, 2025)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Records mentioning, relating to, or generated as a result of investigation or inquiry into civil rights activist and Air Force Maj. Gen. Joseph Alfred McNeil (March 25, 1942 – September 4, 2025). His death is widely-known and widely reported; as such, privacy concerns under FOIA Exemptions 6 and 7 are voided, as are any concerns under the Privacy Act. For example, you may find his New York Times obituary at the following link: https://www.nytimes.com/2025/09/05/us/joseph-mcneil-dead.html

Please conduct a search of the Central Records System, including but not limited to the Electronic Surveillance (ELSUR) Indices, the Microphone Surveillance (MISUR) Indices, the Physical Surveillance (FISUR) Indices, and the Technical Surveillance (TESUR) Indices, for both main-file records and cross-reference records of both HQ and all field offices for all relevant names, agencies, organizations, companies and events including but not limited to those cited in the previous paragraphs and/or links as well as a cross-reference with the Southern Poverty Law Center to include any information provided by the SPLC. My request includes but is not limited to 137, 157, 176, 177, 183, 184, 188, and 214 files. If previously released records are available, then I request a rolling release consisting of those records while additional records are located and processed for release.

If any part of these documents are exempt from disclosure, please be sure to review and release all reasonably segregable nonexempt portions of responsive documents, as the FOIA requires.

I request that I be properly classified as a media requester. I am an assistant editor at Reason Magazine who has written numerous articles on topics of public interest, and I am requesting these documents in an editorial capacity. Please contact me if you have any questions about my media status.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Matthew Petti

Supporting documentation

**

Please be advised that efoia@subscriptions.fbi.gov is a no-reply email
address. Questions regarding your FOIA request may be directed to
foipaquestions@fbi.gov. If you have received a FOIPA request number, please
include this in all correspondence concerning your request. Please note eFOIPA
requests are processed in the order that they are received. If you have not
received a FOIPA request number, your request is in the process of being
opened at which time it will be assigned a FOIPA request number and
correspondence will be forthcoming.

**

Upon receipt of your FOIPA request number, you may check the status of your
FOIPA request on the FBI’s electronic FOIA Library (The Vault) on the FBI’s
public website, http://vault.fbi.gov by clicking on the ‘Check Status of Your
FOI/PA Request tool’ link. Status updates are performed on a weekly basis. If
you receive a comment that your FOIPA request number was not located in the
database, please check back at a later date.

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Nevada Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Short Description of Request: Requesting the incident report from Sparks Police Department
My son Nathan A. Bronfman (34yrs) passed away and was found in his car in Sparks this past November. I am requesting the police report regarding the investigation and findings.

Nathan Adriaan Bronfman (deceased)
Born: May 12th, 1991
Nationality: US and Netherlands
Location: Outside of 6155 Telehurst Dr. Sparks, NV 89434
Police Case Number: 25-8127

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

Good day, 
 
Your request: 20260789 - 25-8127 | REQUEST FOR REPORT NATHAN A. BRONFMAN DECEASED NOV. 8, 2025 is now completed. 
 
 The record(s) you have been provided do not contain any documentation from other agencies or any confidential information.  Sparks Police Department  is required to redact or remove all personal identifying information (NRS 603A.040), proprietary information (NRS 333.335), information from the National Crime Information Center (28 CFR § 50.12(b); USC 28 § 534(f)(1); CJIS Security Policy Section 4.2.3), information about ongoing criminal investigations (NRS 199.520; Donrey of Nevada v. Bradshaw, 106 Nev. 630, 798 P.2d 144 (1990) ), information about victims of crimes (Nevada Constitution, Article 1, Section 23; NRS 178.5691); and information relating to individuals’ private interests such that disclosure to the public would constitute a clearly unwarranted invasion of privacy (Clark Cnty. Sch. Dist. v. Las Vegas Review-Journal, 134 Nev. 700, 429 P.3d 313 (2018)). 
 
 If your request contained any of the above information, it has been redacted or removed as required by law. 
 
Electronic records are available here for the next 60 days: 
https://app.sharebase.com/#/folder/145292/share/242-iajmx6Jsno-lISka8-x6g4kpNF4 
 
If you receive “Invalid Source” when attempting to view a document, download the document and open it in a separate window.

Best regards.

title: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions

communications.subject: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Oregon Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to Oregon Public Records Law, I respectfully request access to records related to the disclosure of employee disability- or accommodation-related information to third-party vendor Grand River Solutions by the University of Oregon.

This request is submitted in the public interest and relates to the potential unauthorized sharing of sensitive medical and ADA-related records concerning a civil rights complaint, during a period of protected activity under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and ORS 659A.

Specifically, I am requesting:

1. Contracts and Agreements
All contracts, MOUs, scopes of work, or other service agreements between the University of Oregon and Grand River Solutions executed or in effect from January 1, 2024 to present.

2. Disclosure Authorizations
Any records, approvals, or internal communications authorizing the disclosure of employee disability, accommodation, or civil rights complaint information to Grand River Solutions, including but not limited to any records referencing:

 * “Kelvin Shivers II” or “Kelvin Shivers” or “Kelvin" 

3. Internal Legal or Risk Review
Any communications between OICRC, HR, General Counsel, or external consultants regarding the legality, advisability, or risk of disclosing employee medical or accommodation-related records to Grand River Solutions.

4. Communications With Grand River Solutions
All emails or written communications between any University of Oregon employee and any representative of Grand River Solutions from January 1, 2025 to present that reference:

   * “Kelvin Shivers II” or “Kelvin Shivers” or “Kelvin"
   * “ADA” or “accommodation”
   * “civil rights complaint” or “retaliation” 

5. Policies or Training Materials
All institutional policies, training materials, or written guidance regarding the sharing of employee ADA, accommodation, or medical information with third-party consultants or investigators, in effect from January 1, 2024 to present.

6. Redacted Copy of Shared Record
If permissible, a redacted copy of any employee-related records provided to Grand River Solutions that reference Kelvin Shivers II, with personally identifying medical information removed to the extent required by law.

I am also formally requesting that the University of Oregon preserve all records related to my ADA accommodation requests, disability disclosures, civil rights complaints, and any internal or external communications referencing me or these matters.

This includes—but is not limited to—records held by the Office of Investigations and Civil Rights Compliance (OICRC), HR/ELR, Workplace ADA, General Counsel, School of Music and Dance, Oregon Bach Festival and any third-party vendors (e.g., Grand River Solutions).

This request is submitted in the public interest. It relates directly to a matter involving potential civil rights violations, ADA-protected accommodations, and government transparency. The requested records concern the treatment of a disabled employee, and their disclosure will contribute to public understanding of institutional compliance with state and federal law.

As such, I respectfully request that any fees be waived in accordance with ORS 192.324(5), which permits fee waivers when disclosure is in the public interest and is not primarily for personal benefit.

This request is time-sensitive and directly relates to an ongoing civil rights matter. If portions of the requested records are exempt from disclosure, please release all segregable non-exempt material as required by ORS 192.338. If you deny any part of this request, please provide a written explanation citing the specific legal exemption(s) relied upon.

Please note: This request and any related public disclosures constitute legally protected activity under ADA Title V (42 U.S.C. § 12203), ORS 659A.109, and the First Amendment. Any adverse action or administrative interference in response to this request may be considered unlawful retaliation and will be documented accordingly.

Thank you for your attention to this request. I am willing to discuss ways to narrow the scope if necessary to facilitate timely release.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Kelvin Shivers II

communications.subject: RE: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
05/19/2025

Dear Mr. Shivers:

The University of Oregon, Office of Public Records has received your public records request for "1. Contracts and Agreements All contracts, MOUs, scopes of work, or other service agreements between the University of Oregon and Grand River Solutions executed or in effect from January 1, 2024 to present. 2. Disclosure Authorizations Any records, approvals, or internal communications authorizing the disclosure of employee disability, accommodation, or civil rights complaint information to Grand River Solutions, including but not limited to any records referencing: * “Kelvin Shivers II” or “Kelvin Shivers” or “Kelvin" 3. Internal Legal or Risk Review Any communications between OICRC, HR, General Counsel, or external consultants regarding the legality, advisability, or risk of disclosing employee medical or accommodation-related records to Grand River Solutions. 4. Communications With Grand River Solutions All emails or written communications between any University of Oregon employee and any representative of Grand River Solutions from January 1, 2025 to present that reference: * “Kelvin Shivers II” or “Kelv" on 05/12/2025, attached.
​
If documents exist and can be provided, the office will send the documents electronically to avoid a copy fee of 25 cents per page. The office also charges for the actual cost of making public records available. The charge includes, but is not limited to, staff costs for locating, gathering, summarizing, compiling, reviewing, tailoring or redacting the public records to respond to a request. The charge may also include the cost of time spent by an attorney in reviewing the public records, redacting material from the public records, or segregating the public records into exempt and nonexempt records.

The cost of time for each employee is calculated by multiplying the employee's hourly wage calculation (including benefits expenses) by the hours or portions thereof necessary to locate, gather, summarize, compile, tailor, review, redact, segregate, certify or attend the inspection of the public records requested.

Thank you for contacting the University with your request.
​
Sincerely,


[cid:image001.png@01DBC8CE.F1B1EF70]

Office of Public Records
6207 University of Oregon | Eugene, OR 97403-6207
(541) 346-6823 | pubrec@uoregon.edu<mailto:pubrec@uoregon.edu>

publicrecords.uoregon.edu<https://publicrecords.uoregon.edu/>

communications.subject: RE: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
Dear Office of Public Records,

Thank you for confirming receipt of my public records request.

Please note that I am a classified staff member, not faculty, so I respectfully request that this request not be misclassified as a faculty record. I am also the subject of the records in question—specifically regarding the disclosure of medical and accommodation information to Grand River Solutions—yet I was never informed of nor consented to this disclosure.

Given the nature of the request, which concerns the University’s obligations under the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, I believe this request serves a clear public interest. I therefore request that you confirm whether a fee waiver will be applied under ORS 192.324(5).

Lastly, please provide an estimated timeline for fulfillment as required under ORS 192.329(5).

Best regards,
Kelvin Shivers II

communications.subject: RE: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
Hi there,

Just checking in on the status of this request. I submitted it some time ago and wanted to see if there’s any update or estimated timeline for a response.

Thanks so much,
Kelvin

communications.subject: RE: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
Dear Records Officer,

Thank you for your response. However, this request has now been pending for nearly two months with no records produced. That is unacceptable.

I first began seeking these records on May 1, 2025, the same day I was notified that my private, disability-related information had been improperly disclosed to Grand River Solutions. I formally submitted this public records request on May 10, 2025. Since then, despite the serious legal implications, I’ve received no records, no legal justification for the delay, and no explanation of how the disclosure was authorized.

This request is not vague, overbroad, or unreasonable. It is specific, time-bound, and clearly necessary to understand how my medical, psychiatric, and disability-related information was released without my consent. It directly relates to potential violations of:
•	34 CFR § 104.36 (Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act) — requires confidentiality of disability-related records maintained for accommodations or services.
•	28 CFR §§ 35.130 & 35.160 (Title II of the ADA) — prohibits disability discrimination and requires accessible, protected communication with individuals known to have disabilities.
•	Office for Civil Rights (OCR) Guidance — requires institutions to promptly investigate and notify affected individuals when disability-related data is breached.
•	ORS 192.355(1) — exempts from disclosure any records containing “medical or psychological histories or diagnoses” where release would be an unreasonable invasion of privacy.

Together, these laws prohibit disclosing disability-related records without informed consent or lawful justification. In my case, the University released letters from my healthcare providers without my knowledge or approval and has yet to explain how this was legal.

To clarify the urgency and history, here is a timeline of key events:
•	Jan 8, 2025: OICRC conducted my interview while I was experiencing known cognitive impairment.
•	Jan 24, 2025: OICRC issued a determination declining to investigate, ignoring context and evidence.
•	Jan 23 – Feb 14, 2025: I attempted repeatedly to understand their decision — my questions were evaded.
•	Feb 14 – Apr 14, 2025: OICRC ignored all further follow-up.
•	Apr 14, 2025: I requested access to the interview video. This request was delayed for over two months.
•	May 1, 2025: I was informed that my medical and psychiatric information had been shared with a third party without consent.
•	May 29, 2025: I received a transcript, but still no video. I was told it would follow the next week — it didn’t.
•	June 30, 2025: I was finally permitted to review the video — nearly three months after requesting it.
•	July 2, 2025: The Oregon State Bar followed up with General Counsel regarding possible attorney misconduct into this very situation.

The video confirmed what the transcript obscured: I was visibly impaired — forgetting words, struggling to communicate, and showing clear signs of distress. The investigator pressed forward anyway, then ignored key evidence and protected activities, and reframed my experiences to justify dismissing the complaint. 

OICRC has confirmed it will only investigate the investigator who exploited my cognitive state, the chief investigator who blocked transparency, and the attorney who helped isolate me from understanding how my records were mishandled — but only if I agree to a third-party vendor chosen and contracted by the University itself. In other words, they are refusing to investigate unless I accept terms that allow them to outsource accountability to an entity not bound by the same transparency and accountability standards required of public institutions.

Under the ADA (Title II) and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, investigations into disability-related grievances must be neutral, accessible, and meaningful. Using a third-party vendor without the employee’s informed consent — especially one selected and paid by the institution under investigation — fails to meet those legal standards. 

Given this, I need a clear understanding of the relationship between the University of Oregon and these third-party investigators before deciding whether to allow such a process to proceed. I am requesting full transparency regarding the University’s agreements with Grand River Solutions or any other third-party vendor, including all records detailing their role, scope, procedures, and any involvement in or advice related to the prior misconduct. Specifically, I need to know whether Grand River Solutions played a part in shaping, advising on, or justifying decisions that contributed to these civil rights violations. Without full disclosure, the process fails to meet the legal requirements for neutrality, transparency, and timeliness under federal and state law. If I cannot verify that Grand River Solutions (or any other 3rd party vendor) is neutral, transparent, free from prior conflicts, and compliant with all applicable legal standards, the University of Oregon remains obligated to conduct its own investigation (in-house) regardless.

It is telling that, after months of delay, the only record your office has produced is a letter from the Lane County District Attorney — a letter that fails to address the University’s ongoing refusal to justify its claimed “faculty” exemption. Instead of responding to my June 5 email from my University account—where I clearly stated I had already received the DA’s determination and was waiting for other inquiries to play out, since the letter didn’t clarify who the faculty member was and appeared to rely on potentially misleading information—your office posted that same letter publicly to my MuckRock request. You knew I already had the document. What I sought was transparency, not a redundant release that conveniently supports the University’s narrative. Worse, the letter was posted with my unredacted contact information, exposing me to potential retaliation. This raises serious concerns and has been documented for oversight agency review.

Meanwhile, your office has not produced or acknowledged:
•	The Oregon State Bar’s July 2 follow-up letter;
•	Any record explaining the legal basis for the records breach;
•	Any internal steps taken to assess the breach or prevent recurrence.

Given the harm already caused — and the University’s legal obligations under ORS Chapter 192, the ADA, and Section 504 — I’m reiterating my request that these records be produced without further delay. Continued obstruction only raises more questions about the lawfulness of the University’s actions and its commitment to transparency, equity, and civil rights compliance.

Sincerely,
Kelvin Shivers II

communications.communication:
To Public Records:

This is a follow-up to your July 25, 2025 response closing my May 10, 2025 public records request.

For Item 4: Please search the following individuals for responsive records:

- Nicole Commissiong - Office of Investigations and Civil Rights Compliance (OICRC)
- Brittany Jayne - Employee Labor Relations
- Jeslyn Everitt - Office of General Counsel
- Mark Schmelz - Human Resources
- Celia Marie Nittmann - Workplace ADA
- Kevin Reed - Office of General Counsel
- Shane Lesher - Office of Investigations and Civil Rights Compliance (OICRC)
- Nick Sloss - Campus Planning and Facilities Management 
- Judith Risch - UO Contractor: Grand River Solutions
- Jackie Hennard - UO Contractor: Grand River Solutions
- Deborah Berman-Montaño - Office of Investigations and Civil Rights Compliance (OICRC)
- Jasmine Narang - Office of General Counsel
- If any other individuals in those offices were copied on relevant communications or played a role in access, review, or coordination, they should be included in the search.

For Item 6:

You claimed no records were “provided” to Grand River Solutions but acknowledged that access was granted. That constitutes disclosure. Your own documents confirm that Jackie Hennard, a Grand River Solutions employee and a risk mitigation contractor the University engaged, was granted access to Maxient folders and OneDrive files containing my complaint and appeal-related materials, which included medically protected information. She confirmed this in writing on April 23 and May 5, 2025, stating she had reviewed the uploaded files and had access to subfolders containing communications between me and Nicole Commissiong. She also asked Nicole for additional emails to help her evaluate whether I had “specifically requested” an appeal or review. Nicole confirmed those emails were in the folder and later coordinated the removal of Jackie’s access. Jackie subsequently confirmed in writing that she had not retained any files, but by that point the disclosure had already occurred and cannot be undone.

Please confirm:

1. What systems or folders GRS accessed (e.g., Maxient, OneDrive)
2. Who provided and/or authorized the access
3. When access was granted and revoked
4. Whether any audit logs or access records exist
5. Please confirm that you are reopening the request and will provide a full and updated response.

Regarding your citation of ORS 192.345(2): 

You did not identify what specific records were withheld under this exemption. I am the subject of the records in question. This statute does not apply to internal documentation about the disclosure or access to my own medical, ADA, or complaint records. Please identify any records withheld under this exemption and explain how they qualify.

Thanks,
Kelvin Shivers II

UO Public Records Office,

It’s now November 7, and I haven’t received any updates or records since your last message. This request has been pending since May 10, 2025 — 181 days — and your office stated you would provide an update no later than August 22, 2025 (77 days ago).

I am actively defending myself in related proceedings, and your prolonged inaction is directly contributing to ongoing harm. Please provide the requested records immediately, explain the unreasonable delays, and waive any associated costs pursuant to ORS 192.324(5), as this request is clearly in the public’s best interest to understand how these processes are being used to suppress the rights of disabled UO employees, as well as their ability to protect themselves from further harm.

This isn’t merely a records request delay, it’s an act of procedural retaliation. By withholding evidence for more than six months, the University has deliberately hindered my ability to respond to false allegations, document ongoing discrimination, and protect my health and rights. This concealment has shielded the same administrators under investigation and enabled further retaliation to continue unchecked, culminating in my layoff and demotion this Fall. Under ORS 659A.109, ORS 659A.199, and ORS 659A.885, this constitutes unlawful interference with protected activity. Please fulfill this request immediately; there is no reasonable justification for any further delay.

Best,
Kelvin Shivers II

communications.subject: RE: Oregon Public Records Law Request: Records Request – University of Oregon Disclosure of ADA Records to Grand River Solutions
communications.communication:
Hello Records Office,

Thank you for your response and for clarifying the timing of the follow-up. I am documenting my understanding of what has been provided so far and need additional clarification so I can reconcile your findings with the information I have already received from the university.

As you noted, the university previously produced some emails between Nicole Commissiong and Grand River Solutions. Those records confirm that GRS was given access to my disability-related and complaint-related information, because they included my investigative file, which contained my medical records, disability disclosures, and doctors’ notes describing how my disabilities are affected in the workplace. In your most recent response, however, you indicate that there are no responsive records containing my name for the broader set of employees I listed, and that you cannot provide records relating to other employees. Taken together, this appears to mean that the only written communications the university is able or willing to produce regarding GRS’s access to my disability information are those involving Nicole, and that no additional communications with the other listed individuals will be disclosed if they exist.

My concern is that this leaves a significant gap in the record. I am trying to understand why and how my disability records and ADA-related information were shared with a third-party risk mitigation firm without my knowledge or consent, and why the university has told me that it is allowed to do so and that nothing improper occurred, when every federal and state law and oversight guidance I have reviewed indicates the opposite. These disclosures occurred only after I asked why my civil rights complaint and related protected activities appeared to have been excluded from review, why key evidence was omitted from the determination, and why I was not provided appeal rights (no appeal was requested at the time, only asked why I was not given the opportunity) despite participating in a 2.5-hour interview (during a documented cognitive relapse) that was then used to decide that no investigation would occur. Under the university’s own procedures, this type of fact-gathering interview is described as part of the investigation process, which makes both the denial of appeal rights and the external transmission of my disability records particularly concerning.

As I mentioned earlier, federal and state law impose specific confidentiality and accountability requirements when disability-related information is disclosed. Under the ADA and Section 504, disability and medical information may only be shared on a need to know basis, and the employer must be able to account for who accessed the information, when it was accessed, under what authority, and for what purpose. These obligations are reflected in 42 USC 12112(d), 29 CFR 1630.14, and parallel Section 504 confidentiality standards. Oregon’s disability and employment laws, including ORS 659A.112 through 659A.139 and ORS 659A.133, similarly require that medical and ADA-related information be kept confidential and disclosed only to individuals with a legitimate role and justification. Oregon’s employee records statute, ORS 652.750, requires employers to maintain and provide records that are used to determine or affect the terms and conditions of employment, which would include records reviewed by Grand River Solutions if their input was used to inform decisions relating to my complaints and employment status.

I understand that ORS 192.345(23) may allow the university to withhold certain system audit logs. My questions are not about the underlying security design of those systems, but about the basic chain of custody that federal and state disability laws require the university to be able to explain. At this point, I know that GRS received my disability and complaint records, I have some of the communications involving Nicole and GRS from your earlier production, and I have written statements from university officials confirming that GRS was involved in reviewing my matter. I do not, however, have any visibility into how that disclosure was authorized beyond Nicole’s involvement, whether other individuals were part of the decision-making process, or why these records were shared in response to my questions about why my complaint had been narrowed or excluded. I also need to understand when GRS first accessed or received my disability information, and whether that access occurred only during the period referenced in the emails you provided or whether any access occurred earlier, including in December 2024 or January or February 2025. In addition, because ADA confidentiality rules require that disability-related information only be disclosed with proper authorization or consent, I need to understand whether the university presented GRS with any document, statement, or representation that it considered to be my consent, and if so, what that consisted of. GRS, as a firm that represents itself as having ADA and civil rights expertise, would typically require clear authorization before accessing disability information, so understanding what was provided to them is necessary for me to reconcile the disclosure with the confidentiality obligations and appeal procedures that apply to ADA-related records.

I am only trying to understand the basic transparency required under state law, the ADA, and Section 504. Within the framework of the records the university is able to identify and disclose, I am trying to understand how the university is accounting for the decision to share my disability records with a third-party risk mitigation firm, and why there appear to be no additional communications involving the other listed employees that would explain or document that decision. If there are categories of records, communication channels, or transmission methods that fall outside the parameters of the search you conducted, or if there is clarifying documentation that explains how the disclosure was authorized and why it was handled in this way, that information would help me reconcile the existing record with the university’s stated position.

Thank you for your time and for helping ensure that my documentation is accurate.
Kelvin Shivers II

communications.communication:
Dear Office of Public Records,

I am writing in response to your January 14, 2026 email stating that the only responsive records that exist involving communications with Grand River Solutions are those between AVP Nicole Commissiong and Grand River Solutions, which were produced on July 25, 2025, and that none of the other employees I identified had communications with Grand River Solutions that referenced me or used my name.

I am requesting clarification and reconsideration based strictly on the scope of my May 10, 2025 request and the standards applied in ordinary Oregon public records practice.

1) The May 10, 2025 request included categories that are broader than direct communications with Grand River Solutions

My May 10 request sought six categories of records. Several of those categories are not limited to direct communications with Grand River Solutions, including:
•	Item 2 (Disclosure Authorizations): “Any records, approvals, or internal communications authorizing the disclosure…” (including but not limited to records referencing my name).
•	Item 3 (Internal Legal or Risk Review): “Any communications between OICRC, HR, General Counsel, or external consultants regarding the legality, advisability, or risk…”
•	Item 5 (Policies or Training Materials): “All institutional policies, training materials, or written guidance…”

Your January 14 response addresses only a narrow interpretation of Item 4, and does not explain how Items 2, 3, and 5 were searched and resolved, beyond the July 25 statement that you were “unable to locate” responsive records for those items.

2) Item 4 is keyword-bounded, but your explanation still requires clarity on how the search was performed

I recognize that Item 4 of my May 10 request is limited to communications between UO employees and Grand River Solutions from January 1, 2025 to present that reference my name and/or the terms “ADA,” “accommodation,” “civil rights complaint,” “retaliation.”

Your January 14 response asserts that no such communications exist beyond the Nicole Commissiong communications already produced. If that is your position, I need clarification of the search basis used to reach that conclusion.

3) Clarification required to reconcile your “no additional records exist” position with the May 10 request scope

Please provide one of the following:

A) Supplemental production (if additional records exist)
Produce any records responsive to Items 2, 3, and 5 (and any additional Item 4 records meeting the stated keyword criteria) that were not included in the July 25 production.

B) If you maintain no additional responsive records exist, provide a records-office-standard description of the search
If you maintain that no additional responsive records exist for Items 2, 3, 5 (and no additional Item 4 communications meeting the stated criteria exist), please briefly describe:
•	what record systems were searched for each item (e.g., email archives, shared drives/SharePoint/OneDrive locations maintained by the relevant offices, Maxient record stores where applicable, etc.);
•	the date ranges applied; and
•	the search terms/methods used that were reasonably calculated to locate records responsive to each item.

4) Exemptions and segregability

In addition, where exemptions were applied (including but not limited to ORS 192.345(2) and ORS 192.345(23)), please:
•	identify the category of record(s) withheld or redacted under each exemption; and
•	confirm that all reasonably segregable non-exempt portions were released, consistent with ORS 192.338.

This request is for clarification and reconciliation of your response with the original May 10 request scope, not an expansion of that request.

Sincerely,
Kelvin Shivers II

title: Sheriff use of force data (2017-2025) (Gloucester County Sheriff's Office)

communications.subject: New Jersey Open Public Records Act Request: Sheriff use of force data (2017-2025) (Gloucester County Sheriff's Office)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the New Jersey Open Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Use of force (UOF) data dating from January 1, 2017 to December 31, 2025. If possible, please provide this data in database or spreadsheet format and include the case number, incident date, incident time, subject injured indicator, subject injury force related info (e.g. Yes or No), subject age, subject gender, subject ethnicity, subject weapon type, incident area occurred, force type, officer division (e.g. law enforcement operations, detention/correctional operations, etc.), badge number and names associated with the officers involved. If one or more of these fields or dates is unavailable, provide all fields and/or information that is available responsive to my request. If no database or spreadsheet format is available, please provide copies of each individual use of force report or other report where use of force information is documented/reported by officers. Note, this is for all use of force incidents reported by your agency, not limited to only those incidents required to be reported under state or federal programs.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 7 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Samuel Sinyangwe

title: Boulder County Flock ALPR

communications.subject: RE: Colorado Open Records Act Request #R000116-011526
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Colorado Open Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

1. ALPR Locations: Please provide the precise location of each Flock Safety ALPR camera deployed and utilized by Boulder County Sheriff's Office.
2. Email Communications with Flock Safety (specifically emails sent to and received from email addresses ending in “@flocksafety.com”): All metadata(from address, to address(es), cc address(es), bcc address(es), date/time sent, date/time received, subject line, and names of all attachments. This request does not seek the contents of the emails themselves, only the specified metadata.) of email communications between Boulder County Sheriff's Office and Flock Safety between January 1, 2021 and the present.
3. Hotlist Criteria: Please provide documentation of policies detailing the criteria and procedures for creating and verifying entries on hotlists in Flock Safety's ALPR system. Specifically:
a. Criteria for adding entries to hotlists (e.g., offense types, jurisdictional warrants, etc.)
b. Verification and approval processes before hotlist alerts are actionable
4. Data-Sharing Agreements: List of all agencies with whom Boulder County Sheriff's Office shares ALPR data, both within Colorado and out-of-state, including federal entities.
5. Policies on ALPR Use and Oversight: Any formal policies or guidelines related to:
a. Acceptable and prohibited uses of ALPR data, including specific restrictions and permissible use cases
b. Access policy, including what constitutes a “valid reason” for system access
c. Human verification procedures for hotlist alerts before law enforcement action
6. Access Logs and Audits: Any access logs, audits, compliance reports, or oversight records evaluating the use, accuracy, and policy compliance of Boulder County Sheriff's Office ALPR systems between January 1, 2021 and the present.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 3 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Matthew Spratlin

communications.subject: Records Request :: R000116-011526
communications.communication:
Dear MATTHEW SPRATLIN: 
We appreciate your interest in criminal justice records of the Boulder County Sheriff’s Office. Your request was received on  1/15/2026 and will be processed according to the Colorado Criminal Justice Records Act (CCJRA), C.R.S. 24-72-301 et seq., and other applicable laws. Disclosure of records involving children may be restricted by the Children’s Code Records and Information Act, C.R.S. 19-1-301 et seq. 
The Sheriff’s Office Records Section does not process requests for public records under the Colorado Open Records Act (CORA), C.R.S. 24-72-201 et seq. Requests for public records under CORA should be submitted to the Boulder County’s Open Records Center available at https://bouldercounty.gov/records/colorado-open-records-act/. (https://u8387778.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=u001.VEsWrlMiJDg0mXafq1mEXaWhAOeQS4p-2FTifjFiHmIBToUhCNwPrijCljaecY3mINRUTFikS2djaQyg-2FJ7hIZ8luPa63HTgmuk3H1tWjGbKI-3DQW5S_Y6hkOFzc7Gmm3-2Bi7zo-2BZvuu4uIOBSkIp7jMmXKFRuYHJRlwdqXH86OpLg0WOws0MTIxkdrrxCuNEvvOPj2QMFlM-2FT-2FZ7glL-2BBqYg9N4wYHZ8lWuxVeAa-2B7P3ulgRA8fuExOEDO32UGNLt8gJiNNi-2FTmlOXJMMM5C6bP7PaJw-2F7l8wABoXl3wkVYCMFqPMD8UbKoFrnP9oUF-2FgVIN352e055NnrnUqbc7SpPbnSvla8Q-3D) 
You will receive an email when the response to your request is ready. While the Records Section is working diligently to respond, please note that the 3-day timeframe for inspection under CORA does not apply to requests for criminal justice records under the CCJRA. This request can be tracked at the link below using assigned reference number R000116-011526. 
The Sheriff’s Office also publishes certain criminal justice information online, including daily booking and listing reports for the Boulder County Jail, available at: https://bouldercounty.gov/safety/jail/listing-and-booking-reports/. (https://u8387778.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=u001.VEsWrlMiJDg0mXafq1mEXaWhAOeQS4p-2FTifjFiHmIBT2MJBJQmvdwUDiv0zYVq10kivwqXhRz5ZRo0NzoQrJNkh6yFBa-2FP7KQLm7eFSs948-3DpP8T_Y6hkOFzc7Gmm3-2Bi7zo-2BZvuu4uIOBSkIp7jMmXKFRuYHJRlwdqXH86OpLg0WOws0MTIxkdrrxCuNEvvOPj2QMFmKLNulZn-2FRuB2-2FuXNkTTOzLJFJlIucpmrnUfewSM76Sk5iGKQX7kfEvKZzeodUz-2B28lQGCUaemLEQi91br7cP1GeS8gScJHiYoQgC9C-2FbVNq9vqZJ2XXtctyOajwSoXHsSeZs8Sfzf9ox88G5zqe3A-3D) 
Thank you for using the records request center. 
Boulder County Sheriff’s Office 
 
To monitor the progress or update this request please log into the Records Request Center (https://u8387778.ct.sendgrid.net/ls/click?upn=u001.VEsWrlMiJDg0mXafq1mEXZ1-2FPdBW8Q1BjPLvu8R38Ih5Vs8D-2FKYdTSTFPiP9LlOewvdyD75zx-2BhdIuphLSZ6TqXMxuN-2FiP-2Bw91l3ufz2YtM-3D8nnO_Y6hkOFzc7Gmm3-2Bi7zo-2BZvuu4uIOBSkIp7jMmXKFRuYHJRlwdqXH86OpLg0WOws0MTIxkdrrxCuNEvvOPj2QMFoeMMA8pEbbxYJpyqpbcTWolVjBDs0ycj2j-2F10UphRtdLkdDM-2FW2aUdGFB2oHiQfAnAPIE3o8NS7AWxWa-2FfBQxqJhSe80nM4OYefGSR5IqlIMzEfr9Rb7zIH8k73RYo-2B6-2FhWSFAd0HmJm3Ij7AZq32M-3D)
